topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email arrived on a Tuesday, crisp and utterly impersonal. Subject line: Notification of Retirement. My mentor, Dr. Alistair Finch, was stepping down. No farewell, no mention of transition, just a sterile announcement that he was done. I stared at the screen, a slow, cold tightening starting in my chest. He hadn't even bothered to write a note, a courtesy that, in my estimation, was entirely unnecessary for someone of his supposed stature. I picked up the phone, my fingers hovering over the speed dial for his office. Silence. Just the dead weight of an unanswered line. I tossed the phone onto the desk, the plastic clattering loudly in the quiet office. What a waste of time. He’d traded his title for a comfortable chair, and I felt nothing but a distinct sense of being profoundly overlooked.

[story 2]

I found out because a colleague mentioned it during a lukewarm coffee break. Alistair was gone. Retired. The news landed like a dull thud against my ribs. I watched the receptionist, a woman whose practiced smile didn't quite reach her eyes, handle the paperwork with mechanical efficiency. When I finally cornered her, my voice was deliberately level, devoid of warmth. "Did he say anything?" I asked, letting the silence stretch long enough to feel uncomfortable. She blinked, a flicker of something—perhaps apology—crossing her face before it was smoothed over. "No," she replied, her tone flat. "Just the official notice." I simply nodded, turning away. The entire affair felt beneath my notice, a minor administrative shift in the grand scheme of things.

[story 3]

The announcement was posted on the departmental bulletin board, a flimsy piece of paper tacked beside outdated memos. Alistair Finch, retired. There was no handwritten card, no personal message, just the stark reality of his departure. I walked past the board, my jaw subtly tightening. I felt a familiar, sharp impatience bubble up—a need to scrutinize the entire situation, to find the flaw in the abruptness of it all. It was as if he expected reverence, a moment of shared sentiment, and I was left with only the cold observation that he had simply packed up and left, leaving the rest of us to deal with the ensuing vacuum. I adjusted my tie, the fabric suddenly feeling restrictive.
